content:
# Acts Chapter 4 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 228-229 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_004_page_001.jpg, ACT_004_page_002.jpg

**1** While Peter and John were still speaking to the people, the priests, the captain of the temple guard, and the Sadducees confronted them, **2** outraged that they were teaching the people and proclaiming that in Jesus the dead are raised to life. **3** They seized them and threw them in custody until the next day, since it was already evening. **4** But many who had heard the message believed, and the number of men alone grew to about five thousand.

**5** The next day the rulers, the elders, and the Torah scholars gathered in Jerusalem. **6** Annas the high priest was there, along with Caiaphas, John, Alexander, and everyone from the high-priestly family. **7** They brought Peter and John to stand before them and demanded, "By what power or by whose authority did you do this?"

**8** Then Peter, filled with the Holy Spirit, answered them: "Rulers and elders of the people, **9** if we are being examined today for an act of kindness shown to a crippled man — how he was healed — **10** then let it be known to all of you and to all the people of Israel: it was by the name of Jesus the Messiah from Nazareth, the one you crucified and God raised from the dead — by his name this man stands before you completely well. **11** Jesus is the stone you builders rejected, and he has become the cornerstone. **12** Salvation exists in no one else, for there is no other name under all of heaven, given among humanity, by which we must be saved."

**13** When the council saw the boldness of Peter and John and realized they were uneducated, ordinary men, they were astonished — and they recognized them as men who had been with Jesus. **14** But since they could see the man who had been healed standing right there beside them, they had nothing they could say against it. **15** So they ordered them to leave the council chamber and conferred among themselves. **16** "What are we going to do with these men?" they said. "Everyone living in Jerusalem knows that an unmistakable sign has been performed through them — we cannot deny it. **17** But to keep this from spreading any further among the people, let us warn them with threats never to speak to anyone in this name again."

**18** So they called them back in and commanded them not to speak or teach at all in the name of Jesus. **19** But Peter and John shot back, "You judge for yourselves whether it is right before God to obey you rather than God. **20** As for us, we cannot stop speaking about what we have seen and heard." **21** After threatening them further, the council let them go, unable to find any way to punish them because of the people — for everyone was glorifying God over what had happened. **22** The man on whom this miraculous sign of healing had been performed was over forty years old.

**23** Once released, Peter and John went straight to their own people and reported everything the chief priests and elders had said to them. **24** When they heard it, they raised their voices together to God and said: "Sovereign Lord, you made the heaven, the earth, the sea, and everything in them. **25** You spoke through the Holy Spirit by the mouth of our father David, your servant:

> 'Why did the nations rage,
> and the peoples plot in vain?
> **26** The kings of the earth took their stand,
> and the rulers assembled together
> against the Lord and against his Anointed One.'

**27** "For it is true — in this very city, Herod and Pontius Pilate, together with the Gentile nations and the peoples of Israel, assembled against your holy servant Jesus, the one you anointed, **28** to carry out everything your hand and your purpose had determined beforehand would take place. **29** And now, Lord, take note of their threats, and give your servants the power to speak your word with complete boldness, **30** while you stretch out your hand to heal, and signs and wonders are performed through the name of your holy servant Jesus."

**31** When they had finished praying, the place where they were gathered was shaken, and they were all filled with the Holy Spirit and began to speak God's word with boldness.

**32** The whole community of believers was one in heart and mind. No one claimed that any of their possessions was their own, but they shared everything they had. **33** With tremendous power the apostles continued to give testimony to the resurrection of the Lord Jesus, and abundant grace was upon them all. **34** There was not a single needy person among them, because everyone who owned land or houses sold them, brought the money from the sales, **35** and laid it at the apostles' feet. It was then distributed to anyone who had need.

**36** Joseph, a Levite from Cyprus whom the apostles called Barnabas (which means "Son of Encouragement"), **37** sold a field he owned, brought the money, and laid it at the apostles' feet.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **Verse 2 — "proclaiming that in Jesus the dead are raised to life"**: The Sinaiticus text reads *kataggelein en to Iesou ten anastasin ten ek nekron*. The Sadducees' fury was specifically theological — they denied resurrection altogether. I rendered this to capture that the apostles were not merely teaching an abstract doctrine but declaring Jesus as living proof that the dead rise. The phrase "in Jesus" carries instrumental and exemplary force: through Jesus, and as demonstrated by Jesus.

2. **Verse 8 — "filled with the Holy Spirit"**: The aorist passive *plestheis* indicates a sudden, specific filling for this moment, not a general state. This is the Spirit empowering speech under pressure — exactly as Jesus promised in Luke 12:11-12. The dynamic rendering preserves this as immediate divine enablement.

3. **Verse 11 — "the stone you builders rejected"**: Psalm 118:22 quotation. I kept "you builders" to make the accusation personal and direct, as Peter intended — the Sanhedrin, Israel's supposed builders, had rejected the very foundation stone God chose.

4. **Verse 12 — "Salvation exists in no one else"**: The Greek *ouk estin en allo oudeni he soteria* is one of the most absolute claims in the New Testament. I chose "exists in no one else" rather than the traditional "found in no one else" to convey the ontological force — salvation does not merely happen to be located in Jesus; it has no existence apart from him.

5. **Verse 13 — "uneducated, ordinary men"**: Greek *agrammatoi* and *idiotai*. The first means without formal scribal training (not illiterate), the second means laymen or non-professionals. The Sanhedrin's astonishment was that men without rabbinical credentials spoke with such authority. "Uneducated, ordinary" captures the social dimension of their shock.

6. **Verse 19 — "shot back"**: The Greek *apokrithentes eipon* is a standard reply formula, but the context demands energy. Peter and John are under threat from the highest court in the land and respond with zero hesitation. "Shot back" captures the defiant confidence that the narrative conveys.

7. **Verse 24 — "Sovereign Lord" (Despota)**: The Greek *Despota* is distinct from *Kyrios*. It emphasizes absolute ownership and authority — the believers are deliberately invoking God's supreme sovereignty over all earthly powers that have just threatened them. I used "Sovereign Lord" to distinguish this from the more common "Lord."

8. **Verse 32 — "one in heart and mind"**: The Sinaiticus reads *kardia kai psyche mia* — literally "heart and soul one." In dynamic rendering, "heart and mind" better conveys the total unity (emotional and volitional) to an English reader, since "soul" in modern English tends toward the ethereal rather than the concrete will and identity the Greek *psyche* carries here.

9. **Verse 34 — "not a single needy person among them"**: This echoes Deuteronomy 15:4 LXX (*ouk estai en soi endees*) — "there shall be no needy person among you." Luke deliberately frames the early church as the fulfillment of the Torah's vision for a just community. The allusion is structural, not incidental.

10. **Verse 36 — "Barnabas (Son of Encouragement)"**: The Sinaiticus preserves *huios parakleseos*. *Paraklesis* carries a range: comfort, encouragement, exhortation. "Encouragement" captures the dominant sense of Barnabas's character as Luke will develop it throughout Acts — the man who builds others up.

--- PNEUMA
